title: Start Spreading The News

jokes.text: Moira leaving a voicemail for Bev announcing the move to New York, calling her 'getatable' for a 'little red biddy, little back chat'
jokes.setup: Moira is leaving what sounds like a warm social voicemail
jokes.punchline: 'Getatable' is a Moira-invented word; the whole message is delivered in her theatrical, anachronistic register — she's using Bev purely as a gossip pipeline while making it sound like a social call
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira cold open: invented vocabulary ('getatable'), arch social performance, and the immediate reveal that she's weaponizing Bev for gossip. High character integration — only Moira would end a friendly voicemail with 'Ciao' and frame manipulation as warmth.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Are you having lunch with Bev? After everything that happened between you two?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I've decided to amputate that relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You mean cut it off?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, when I amputate, I amputate completely.

jokes.text: 'No, I amputated those people years ago.'
jokes.setup: John asks if she's having lunch with Bev 'after all this'
jokes.punchline: Moira uses the surgical metaphor 'amputated' for cutting people out of her life — as if social excision is a medical procedure she performed
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The word 'amputated' is unexpected, surgical, and perfectly Moira — it implies these former friends were a diseased limb. Four words, maximum comic payload. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in economy and character specificity. The clinical precision of the metaphor against the casual delivery is what elevates it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I simply had to ring her up. One cannot allow misinformation to spread unchecked through the town's collective big bazoo.

jokes.text: 'But bev and her big bazoo will get the word out quicker than any group memorandum.'
jokes.setup: Moira has just explained she 'amputated' Bev years ago
jokes.punchline: She's still strategically using Bev as a gossip vector — 'bazoo' (big mouth) is a vintage Moira-ism; the corporate framing ('group memorandum') for social gossip is absurdly grandiose
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the contradiction — she's using the woman she 'amputated' as a PR tool — and in the Moira-specific diction ('bazoo', 'memorandum'). Slightly diffuses the impact of joke #2 but is a solid follow.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Where's breakfast?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's right here. Vodka, gin, rum.

jokes.text: 'Kids! Breakfast is served.' / 'I already ate.' / 'I don't see any breakfast.' / 'Mimosas! We're celebrating!'
jokes.setup: John announces 'Breakfast is served' with enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: The 'breakfast' is just mimosas — no food — which the family greets with resignation
jokes.explanation: Efficient multi-beat joke: David's 'I already ate' is the first sign something's off, Alexis's 'I don't see any breakfast' is the straight-man beat, and 'Mimosas!' is the punchline. The Rose family's idea of a celebratory breakfast being alcoholic is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Can we please just take the photo? I'm not going to get any happier than this.

jokes.text: 'So there's no food then?'
jokes.setup: Moira announces mimosas as the celebratory breakfast
jokes.punchline: David's flat, resigned confirmation that there is no actual food — delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: David as the family's most grounded appetite — and the deadpan delivery of the obvious truth — is the joke. Simple but well-executed character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This is perfect. A framer.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm deleting it.

jokes.text: John toasts Moira's potential Broadway career — 'or maybe a pledge drive on PBS'
jokes.setup: John is warmly praising Moira, saying 'Broadway will be calling'
jokes.punchline: He undercuts it with 'or maybe a pledge drive on PBS' — a significantly less glamorous fallback that he delivers as if equally impressive
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the casual demotion — John pivots from 'Broadway' to 'PBS pledge drive' without missing a beat, treating them as equivalent. Moira's cheerful 'oh, you are sweet' response adds another layer since she apparently doesn't register the slight.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The air quality here is absolutely pristine. Have you noticed how crisp and clean everything smells?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Everything seems better now that we're leaving.

jokes.text: 'No. I don't think that's a good idea.' / 'Absolutely not.' — David and Alexis refusing to cook
jokes.setup: John suggests the family cook something together as a farewell celebration
jokes.punchline: Both David and Alexis refuse immediately and in unison — the Rose children's domestic helplessness is so established that 'absolutely not' is the only possible answer
jokes.explanation: The joke runs on established character history — the Rose children famously cannot cook. The synchrony of refusal is the comic beat.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know what you're thinking, but hear me out. This truck is an investment. It's got leather seats, heated cup holders, and a sound system that'll make your fillings vibrate.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, didn't you just file for bankruptcy last month?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Exactly. Fresh start. New truck, new Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I think the concern is less about the truck and more about your ability to actually pay for it.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Details.

jokes.text: 'David? You don't have to look quite so morose. You're with your family.'
jokes.setup: The family tries to take a celebratory photo with glasses raised
jokes.punchline: John's gentle note that David is looking 'morose' — with the implied secondary joke that being with his family IS a reason for David to look morose
jokes.explanation: The double meaning — 'you're with your family' as comfort vs. explanation of the moroseness — is the understated joke. Classic Schitt's Creek warmth-with-an-edge.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I went to the spa yesterday and got a pedicure and a blowout at the same time. The aesthetician said, 'Just don't move your feet.' I said, 'Don't worry, my feet are very obedient.' Turns out they're not. I kicked over the nail polish, it went all over my hair, and now I have purple feet and blonde hair with sparkles in it. My husband asked if I was going through a phase.

jokes.text: Moira musing about the air having 'a particularly lovely redolence' and detecting 'a scintilla of lavender'
jokes.setup: The Roses are outside enjoying the morning
jokes.punchline: John deflates it: 'Isn't it funny how the air suddenly smells sweeter now that you know we're leaving' — the 'redolence' is psychological, not real
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical diction ('redolence', 'scintilla') is self-satirizing, and John's grounded correction adds gentle irony. Works as both character comedy and observational humor about the psychology of departure.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm gonna bring some of Jocelyn's famous butter tarts down to the dealership. That salesman's gonna give me the premium package, the extended warranty, the whole nine yards.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, you can't bribe a car salesman with butter tarts.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Why not? Works on me. Works on the mayor. Works on basically everyone in this town.

jokes.text: Roland announces he bought a new truck because 'if you wanna be an executive, you gotta drive like an executive'
jokes.setup: Roland arrives in a shiny new truck
jokes.punchline: His logic for the purchase — 'executive' self-image — is comically inflated for someone of his station, and John's immediate concern about premature spending is the straight-man beat
jokes.explanation: Roland's working-class-guy-plays-executive bit is well-worn but consistent with his character. The 'executive' framing for a pickup truck is the comedic dissonance.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you do something different with your hair?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, I just didn't wash it.

jokes.text: 'I went to the spa. Had a pedicure and a blow-out, at the same time.'
jokes.setup: Roland is describing how he's been spending his new executive salary
jokes.punchline: The simultaneous pedicure and blow-out — the specificity and pride with which Roland announces this luxury multitasking is the joke
jokes.explanation: Roland's enthusiasm for simultaneously having a pedicure and blow-out plays against type — this boorish character treating himself to dual spa services is funny because of who he is. Short and punchy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Johnny, worry is just love with nowhere to go.

jokes.text: 'I went to the spa. Had a pedicure and a blow-out, at the same time.'
jokes.setup: Roland is justifying spending money on the truck; Jocelyn chimes in about her own splurge
jokes.punchline: The 'at the same time' is the punchline — this is Jocelyn's idea of maximum luxury: simultaneous pedicure and blow-out
jokes.explanation: The contrast between the Roses' baseline luxury lifestyle and Jocelyn's idea of extravagance is a recurring class-comedy vein. 'At the same time' lands as the punchline because it's so earnestly delivered as an achievement.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm thinking of getting him something that really captures the New York experience.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Maybe a mugging, or a really aggressive pigeon.

jokes.text: Roland reveals he's been test-driving the truck without telling the salesman — 'I told the salesman I was just taking it around the block'
jokes.setup: Roland appears to have legitimately bought the truck
jokes.punchline: He's just test-driving it and is trying to bribe his way to a sunroof with cinnamon buns
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely funny Roland escalation: the reveal that he hasn't bought the truck is good, but 'maybe some cinnamon buns might just buy us a sunroof' is the real punchline — his confidence that baked goods are viable currency at a car dealership. Strongly in character and subverts the 'executive' setup beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, you're not moving to California?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? No. Why would I move to California?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know, I thought... isn't that where you're going?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going for a trip. A trip, Stevie. Not to relocate my entire life. Why would you assume I'm moving? Why would everyone just uproot their lives and move to California with me?

jokes.text: 'Here, honey, you hold the buns.'
jokes.setup: Roland has just explained the cinnamon bun negotiation strategy
jokes.punchline: He delegates the buns to Jocelyn with executive seriousness — treating pastry bribery as a formal negotiation tactic
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on joke #13. The delegation of bun-holding is absurdly earnest. Short, sharp, well-placed.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I needed to know that I could leave. I didn't actually need to leave. There's a philosophical distinction between needing to do something vs. needing to know you could.

jokes.text: 'Worry is but undernourished enthusiasm.'
jokes.setup: John tells Moira not to worry about 'Sunrise Bay' moving on without her
jokes.punchline: Moira reframes worry as 'undernourished enthusiasm' — a pseudo-philosophical aphorism that sounds profound but is largely meaningless
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable Moira-ism. The joke works on multiple levels: it sounds like a self-help aphorism, it's grammatically interesting, and it's completely characteristic of Moira performing stoicism while barely concealing disappointment. 'Undernourished enthusiasm' is the kind of invented compound phrase that becomes a fan-favorite quote. Scores high on originality and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, no, I didn't... Patrick didn't want... I wasn't supposed to say anything about the houses.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I'm the worst. I'm literally the worst person.

jokes.text: David complimenting Stevie's ponytail as looking 'more professional' — turns out she just didn't wash her hair
jokes.setup: David notices something different about Stevie and diplomatically says she looks 'more professional' — implying he's trying to be complimentary about a new post-deal look
jokes.punchline: 'Is in a ponytail because I didn't wash it'
jokes.explanation: Well-structured: David tries to be complimentary, Stevie deflates it instantly with the mundane truth. His attempt to read meaning into her appearance ('since you got the deal, you've decided to dress more professionally') is a great David move — over-intellectualizing something completely banal. Stevie's flat delivery would make this land hard.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So you're just leaving?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I... well, yes, but I thought we had discussed this.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We didn't discuss anything, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh. Oh no. We need to have a conversation, don't we?

jokes.text: David asking whether a gift card to Joe's Pizza or tickets to 'Wicked' says 'Patrick, you're gonna love New York'
jokes.setup: David is asking Stevie for gift advice for Patrick about New York
jokes.punchline: The two options — Joe's Pizza gift card vs. Wicked tickets — are such a David binary: one extremely lowbrow, one extremely tourist-broadway, both very on-brand
jokes.explanation: Mildly amusing character-comedy — the specificity of 'Wicked' and 'Joe's Pizza' as David's New York frame of reference is a little funny. Not a big joke but adds texture.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh, you brought the garbage?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? No, Twyla, these are my clothes. I'm getting rid of them.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh! Well, they're all designer labels. These are nice things.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm over them.

jokes.text: 'I realized I didn't need to live in a big city. I... guess I just needed to know that I could.'
jokes.setup: David is shocked Stevie isn't moving to New York
jokes.punchline: Stevie's blunt, self-aware articulation of her whole arc — played completely dry
jokes.explanation: This functions as both a character comedy moment and a quietly profound statement. Stevie's self-knowledge delivered deadpan — 'I just needed to know that I could' — is the joke and the emotional truth simultaneously. Very Schitt's Creek.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, Twyla, that's so sweet, but I can't let you do that. I mean, I appreciate it, but... you know what? Fine. But I'm tipping you like we're in a city, because I'm not about to become one of those people who tips poorly just because we're in a small town.

jokes.text: The cascading revelation that Patrick was looking at a house — 'What house?' 'What?' 'What house?'
jokes.setup: Stevie mentions Patrick may have been looking at a house 'for the two of you'
jokes.punchline: David's double 'What house?' — the second one breaking down in disbelief — shows his total ignorance of this development
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the repetition with escalating panic — 'What house? / What? / What house?' — and Stevie's realization mid-sentence that she's done something wrong ('I feel like I'm doing something wrong'). Good structural beat.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I don't waitress for money.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, what do you do it for? Like, are you a serial killer? Do you kill people?

jokes.text: 'So you're finally getting out.' — Stevie to David, delivered with quiet resignation
jokes.setup: The house revelation has just been processed; David is about to leave to find Patrick
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat 'so you're finally getting out' — treating their years in Schitt's Creek like a prison sentence they've both been serving
jokes.explanation: The prison-release framing of leaving Schitt's Creek is a perfectly understated Stevie line. The awkward pause that follows (05:08-05:12) is the real beat — David's '...yeah. Um...' registers the emotional weight under the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I won the lottery.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God! How much did you win?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Seven dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Seven dollars? That's... that's amazing, Twyla!

jokes.text: 'I've seen how people tip here.' — Alexis refuses Stevie's offer of money with this explanation.
jokes.setup: Stevie offers to pay for the clothes she wants to keep
jokes.punchline: Alexis declines money not out of generosity but because she has such contempt for small-town tipping culture
jokes.explanation: Six words that capture Alexis perfectly — her condescension is so reflexive she doesn't realize she's being insulting. It's also a little snide about small-town economics. Economy is the real strength here.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I won the lottery!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, how much?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, it was $92 million, but I had to split it with someone.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh no, that's terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: So I ended up with $46 million.

jokes.text: 'Okay, well, if you don't do this for money, I'm scared to know what you do do for money.'
jokes.setup: Twyla tells Alexis she doesn't do the café job for the money
jokes.punchline: Alexis's alarmed follow-up — 'what you do do for money' — with the double 'do do' landing as mild wordplay, and the implication that Twyla's off-books income is something scandalous
jokes.explanation: The 'do do' is a minor wordplay bonus, but the real joke is the setup for the lottery reveal. Alexis's scandalous implication is a solid character beat — she immediately goes to the darkest possible explanation.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I just think if you have money, you should be responsible with it. You can't just go out and buy a lot of snowmobiles.

jokes.text: 'Can you keep a secret?' / 'Depends on how dark it's gonna get, but yes.'
jokes.setup: Twyla asks if Alexis can keep a secret
jokes.punchline: Alexis's conditional agreement — 'depends on how dark it's gonna get' — is a perfect character beat: she's anticipating something disturbing and isn't fully committing
jokes.explanation: Excellent character line for Alexis — her conditional agreement implies she has heard some deeply dark secrets in her life and needs to manage expectations. The casualness of 'but yes' after hedging is perfect timing. Also works as a great setup for the lottery reveal, which is decidedly not dark.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, what is this? This is... oh no. No, no, no. This is not going to Goodwill.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's just something I wore to... you know what, never mind. It's staying in my closet.

jokes.text: Twyla reveals she won money in the lottery — Alexis gasps — then Twyla says '$92 million' — Alexis nearly faints.
jokes.setup: Twyla has a secret about having won some money in the lottery
jokes.punchline: $92 million — the escalation from 'won some money' to this amount is the laugh
jokes.explanation: The reveal is well-structured: Twyla's casual manner versus the astronomical sum creates a strong comedic contrast. Alexis's reaction ('I literally was about to pass out') is funny and earned.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was perched atop a life-size VHS cassette in a sequined gown, one leg extended, my hair in a elaborate updo. It was very 'Flashdance meets archival technology.'

jokes.text: 'So you won like... s-so much money.' Alexis stuttering as she tries to calculate half of $92 million
jokes.setup: Twyla clarifies she split the $92M with one other person
jokes.punchline: Alexis can't do the math and lands on 'so much money' — her brain buffering on the figure
jokes.explanation: The stutter ('s-so much money') lands because it's specific — Alexis is someone who grew up rich and knows what large sums feel like, and she still can't compute this. The understatement of 'so much money' after '$46 million' is perfectly played.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes. I mean, in the parlance of the affirmative, and with the kind of enthusiasm that one might reserve for a Chanel trunk show or a private audience with the Dalai Lama, I am inclined to acquiesce to your proposition.

jokes.text: 'This cute dress makes me smile.' — Alexis holds up a dress from the donation bag, clearly keeping it
jokes.setup: Alexis has been delivering a heartfelt speech about how 'spending on something special might not buy happiness but can make you smile'
jokes.punchline: She immediately applies the philosophy to justify keeping one of her own donated dresses — the lesson lands on herself
jokes.explanation: The callback to her own advice, immediately applied to her own benefit, is a very Alexis move. The self-serving philosophy is funny because she genuinely doesn't see the irony. 'Not quite sure how that one got in there' is a good button.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, the actress who replaced me had a complete nervous breakdown on set, so they had no choice but to bring me back. I mean, I'd like to think it was my irreplaceable talent that drew them back, but it was really just her mental deterioration that made it convenient.

jokes.text: 'Oh, that photo of me sunbathing atop a giant VHS tape was a staple of Times Square for a whole season.'
jokes.setup: John mentions Moira's past work as the Rose Video spokesperson
jokes.punchline: The visual of Moira sunbathing on a giant VHS tape in Times Square — delivered with complete nostalgic pride
jokes.explanation: The image is gloriously absurd — Moira sunbathing atop a giant VHS tape — and her pride in it as a career achievement is perfect character comedy. The specificity ('a whole season') adds to the joke. This is a visual gag that exists only in dialogue but lands vividly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're giving me the tennis bracelet?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Absolutely not. It's a Cartier. I'm merely allowing you to borrow it for the evening.

jokes.text: 'Even the bracelet?' / 'Even the bracelet.' / 'And you're giving it to me?' / 'No, there are 30 diamonds on that thing.'
jokes.setup: The family learns Moira got everything she demanded, including the bracelet
jokes.punchline: John asks if she's giving it to him as a gift — the answer is an immediate, appalled 'No, there are 30 diamonds on that thing'
jokes.explanation: John's innocent assumption that the bracelet was a gift — and Moira's horrified correction — is a classic marital joke. '30 diamonds on that thing' as the justification is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I didn't want to overwhelm you with information.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I'm a grown man. I can handle a house.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I know, but I thought—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Unless you're talking about an actual house made of food plates. In which case, yes, I'm overwhelmed.

jokes.text: 'Cause I know how flustered you can get when there's more than one thing on your plate.' / 'Hm. Unless it's food.'
jokes.setup: Patrick explains he waited because David gets overwhelmed by too much at once
jokes.punchline: David's immediate, flat amendment: 'Unless it's food' — a rare moment of David self-awareness delivered as deadpan correction
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Patrick's setup is earnest and accurate — David IS easily flustered — and David's one-word amendment ('unless it's food') is brilliant because (1) it's a perfect character exception, (2) David is completely aware of this about himself, (3) it takes the emotional tension and punctures it perfectly. The economy is exceptional — three words do everything.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So you didn't like New York?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I mean, I had a very nice time there. It's just that I was assaulted in a park, robbed twice, and I got hepatitis A. So, you know, mixed bag.

jokes.text: Patrick knocked on a stranger's door after David's fourth comment about the house looking like Kate Winslet's cottage from 'The Holiday'
jokes.setup: Patrick explains why he never mentioned the house — he'd been researching it since David kept mentioning it
jokes.punchline: He knocked on the door of a house that wasn't for sale, on the basis of four drive-bys and a Kate Winslet reference
jokes.explanation: The 'Kate Winslet's cottage from The Holiday' detail is the joke and the heart simultaneously. The specificity of the film reference — a 2006 romantic comedy — tells you everything about who David is. And Patrick acting on something so specific and so small is what makes him the perfect partner for David. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So you're not missing New York?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I'm not missing New York. I spent years there being absolutely miserable, broke, and unappreciated.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: But you're happy here?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Deliriously.

jokes.text: Patrick says 'It's gonna be a tough goodbye' — then notes: 'This is where we started our life together.' Beat. David: 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Patrick delivers a sincere romantic observation about Schitt's Creek
jokes.punchline: David's single-syllable 'Yeah' after a beat — a quiet understatement that's more loaded than any speech
jokes.explanation: More warm than funny, but David's single 'Yeah' is doing a lot of comedic and emotional work simultaneously. On rewatch — knowing he stays — it becomes funnier as you see him processing it in real time.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not going to Yankees games, Patrick. You're going to have to find new friends.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What? Why would I need new friends?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because I'm not interested in baseball, and clearly that's a dealbreaker for you.

jokes.text: 'Why have I not been told about the double-decker sightseeing tour?'
jokes.setup: Moira encounters the Jazzagals who have organized a surprise farewell gesture with a double-decker bus
jokes.punchline: Her first response is administrative outrage — she wasn't informed about the logistics — rather than gratitude
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira — her first reaction to a surprise farewell gesture is not warmth but logistical complaint. The phrasing 'double-decker sightseeing tour' makes it funnier, implying she's been denied a tourist attraction.

dialogue.speaker: Veronica
dialogue.line: This show is absolute garbage! The writing is terrible, the acting is wooden, and I wouldn't watch this sh*t if you paid me!

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh my god, Veronica! Language!

jokes.text: 'I say, fuck 'em.' — Veronica, about Sunrise Bay
jokes.setup: The Jazzagals are rallying around Moira about Sunrise Bay apparently moving on without her
jokes.punchline: 'Fuck 'em' from a woman named Veronica — the bluntness is the joke, especially in the context of the Jazzagals' otherwise gentle group energy
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in the incongruity of Veronica's bluntness vs. the Jazzagals' polite choral group context. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, a wolf pack is only as strong as its weakest link. And together, we are an unstoppable force of nature. We hunt. We gather. We survive. We are the Jazzagals.

jokes.text: 'I mean, who doesn't love a reboot.' — Jazzagal, cheerfully, immediately after Moira announces she got the job
jokes.setup: The Jazzagals were sympathetically trashing Sunrise Bay ('reboots are never as good as the original'); Moira then reveals she got the part
jokes.punchline: One Jazzagal immediately pivots to 'who doesn't love a reboot' — total reversal of the previous group position
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback structure — the same group that was comforting Moira with 'reboots are never good' immediately reverses course the second it benefits her. The speed of the reversal is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've been thinking of myself as a wolf. A lone wolf, if you will. Prowling through the underbrush of life, my senses heightened, my instincts sharp. And then one day, I realize I'm not a wolf at all. I'm a butterfly. No, wait—I'm a phoenix. Actually, I'm a wolf again, but this time with wings. You see, the chrysalis of my former self has shed its skin like a snake, which I also am now.

jokes.text: 'Oh, thanks for the heads up.' — Jazzagal, sarcastically, about Moira's last-minute departure announcement
jokes.setup: Moira announces this will be her last rehearsal with them
jokes.punchline: Dry sarcastic reaction from a Jazzagal — 'camera waits for no one' is also in the mix
jokes.explanation: Competent reaction beat — the sarcasm is deserved and lands — but it's a supporting-character joke with limited specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I appreciate the gesture, I really do. But I'm not going to stand here and cry about my journey or whatever it is you're expecting. I've done enough emotional catharsis on Lifetime television to last me several lifetimes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And frankly, I think we should all be very grateful that I'm not having a complete breakdown in a poorly lit motel room while a concerned love interest looks on.

jokes.text: 'No. I just-I just don't like surprises. We should-we should get started.' — Veronica denying she's crying
jokes.setup: Moira offers Veronica the 'something in your eye' cover; Veronica is clearly crying
jokes.punchline: She denies crying by claiming she 'doesn't like surprises' — a completely implausible explanation — and then pivots to rehearsal logistics
jokes.explanation: The absurd excuse ('I just don't like surprises') followed by a pivot to practicalities is funny. A minor character getting a moment of comedic dignity.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You know, what you said about spending on special things for yourself really resonated with me.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, that's so great!

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: So I took your advice. I bought the café.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, what? No, I meant like a spa day or something.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, this is my spa day now.

jokes.text: Moira's wolf pup metaphor extended: 'wolf pups are born blind and deaf, and in your care I have learned to see and hear the beauty in the bucolic'
jokes.setup: Moira is giving her farewell speech to the Jazzagals
jokes.punchline: She extends the wolf metaphor into an actual biology lesson, which is both absurd and unexpectedly moving
jokes.explanation: Moira committing to the metaphor so hard she includes wolf development facts is a glorious comedy-of-commitment moment. 'The beauty in the bucolic' — the alliteration, the vocabulary — is pure Moira. The line also lands emotionally because it's sincerely how she feels.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You know, what Alexis said really resonated with me. She told me to spend a little money on myself.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: So I quit my job, bought a horse, and I'm moving to Costa Rica.

jokes.text: 'Oh no. No. Let's not turn this into a hallmark movie of the week. I've done enough of those for a lifetime.'
jokes.setup: The farewell is becoming emotional and sentimental
jokes.punchline: Moira breaks the moment by referencing her actual career history — Hallmark movies — as a reason to avoid sentimentality
jokes.explanation: Beautifully meta — Moira's career as a TV actress lets her recognize and reject the genre of scene she's in. 'I've done enough of those for a lifetime' is a genuine laugh because it's simultaneously self-aware about her work AND the show's format. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I'm thinking of calling it 'The Café at the End of the Universe: A Rose by Any Other Name Would Smell as Sweet, But This One Smells Like Opportunity.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What about just calling it 'Twyla's Café'?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: No, that's too complicated.

jokes.text: 'Did I forget to pay for brunch again?' — David to Twyla when she shows up at his door
jokes.setup: Twyla shows up at David's door unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: David's first assumption is that he has a tab to settle — implying this has happened before and is a known David pattern
jokes.explanation: The 'again' is the key word — David has forgotten to pay for brunch enough times that it's his first assumption when Twyla appears. Economical character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, Twyla, I can't accept that. That's way too generous.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm going to regret this immediately, aren't I?

jokes.text: 'Twy. That means so much. Um... What part of what I said, specifically?'
jokes.setup: Twyla says Alexis's words 'really resonated'
jokes.punchline: Alexis's immediate follow-up — asking which part specifically — implies she has no memory of giving good advice and needs to confirm what she said
jokes.explanation: A great Alexis moment: she accepts the compliment ('that means so much') and then immediately asks what she said — because she doesn't remember her own advice and needs to fact-check it. This is funny because it reveals she dispenses wisdom without tracking it, and also because she's immediately suspicious of herself.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's a lovely dress, Twyla. Is it new?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you David. No, it's not new. My friend let me borrow it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, your friend has excellent taste.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Yes, she really does. It's Alexis's.

jokes.text: Twyla gives Alexis a suitcase of cash for her dresses — Alexis realizes it's far too much for the dresses
jokes.setup: Twyla gives Alexis what appears to be a going-away present — payment for the clothes
jokes.punchline: 'Those dresses would never sell for this much, like not even in retail'
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the lottery reveal — Twyla quietly deploying her wealth to help Alexis. The comedy is in Alexis correctly identifying that the offer is wildly disproportionate, and in Twyla calling it 'a goodbye present' so casually.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't understand how you think.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, join the club. I don't understand how I think either.

jokes.text: 'You're gonna do great things, Alexis.' / 'No, I know.'
jokes.setup: Twyla gives Alexis an encouraging send-off
jokes.punchline: Alexis's response: 'No, I know.' — not 'thank you' or 'really?' but confirmation that she agrees with the compliment
jokes.explanation: This might be the most Alexis line in the episode. 'No, I know.' — two words that perfectly capture her character: the unshakeable self-belief, the inability to be surprised by a compliment, and yet delivered without malice. It's funny and weirdly endearing. High character integration; this line ONLY works for Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was making a joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It was meant to be funny.

jokes.text: 'Hey Twy, like, cute dress.' / 'Thanks. My friend gave it to me.'
jokes.setup: Someone compliments Twyla's new dress — one of the dresses from Alexis's bag
jokes.punchline: 'My friend gave it to me' — Twyla is wearing Alexis's rejected dress (the one Alexis kept, then apparently gave), closing the loop
jokes.explanation: A nice structural callback — the dress that Alexis pulled out of the donation bag ('this cute dress makes me smile') ends up on Twyla. The economy of the scene closing beat is good.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Patrick is so thoughtful. He does all these sweet gestures.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, some of us are simply not capable of that level of emotional generosity.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, you literally just said—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm aware of what I said, Alexis. And I stand by my assessment of my own moral deficiencies.

jokes.text: 'Okay, so we just drove here to look at a place that you don't plan on living in?' / 'Correct.'
jokes.setup: Moira parses what David has just told her — he came to look at a house he's already decided not to live in
jokes.punchline: David's single-word 'Correct' — owning the irrationality completely
jokes.explanation: 'Correct.' is a great single-word punchline. David's complete acceptance of his own irrationality — no defense, no explanation — is a strong character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So you want to go back to New York to prove something to the people who rejected you?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't want to prove something to them. I want to prove something to myself.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: By succeeding in front of the people who hurt you?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. Is that... is that wrong?

jokes.text: 'How many years have I known you, and I still can't figure out what goes on inside your head.' / 'Join the club.'
jokes.setup: Moira expresses bafflement at David's behavior
jokes.punchline: David's 'Join the club' — he includes himself in the group that can't understand him
jokes.explanation: The self-inclusion in the confusion club is a good David moment. The beat that follows — 'that was a joke, in case it didn't land' — actually extends it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're all going to Coachella.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Apparently, it's the same weekend as my wedding, and they've already committed to the festival.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Because I want those people to know that I'm not a joke. And that I've won.' David: 'David, look at this place. You've won.'
jokes.setup: Alexis explains why she needs to return to New York — to prove herself to people who dismissed her
jokes.punchline: David uses her own logic against her — gesturing at Schitt's Creek as evidence she's already won — which is both sincere and funny because the town is objectively humble
jokes.explanation: David's gesture at Schitt's Creek as proof of winning is both sweet and gently ironic — the comedy comes from the gap between 'winning' and what Schitt's Creek actually is. The line works simultaneously as sincere character support and gentle self-aware irony.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because I need to prove to myself that I'm not a complete failure. And also, I'd like to prove it to everyone else who said I would be.

jokes.text: 'And on a personal note, I don't want you to leave me here, okay?'
jokes.setup: Moira has been making an eloquent case for David staying
jokes.punchline: She drops the eloquence entirely for the naked admission 'I don't want you to leave me here' — the vulnerability framed by 'on a personal note' as if it's a business meeting
jokes.explanation: 'On a personal note' is the joke — Moira has been rhetorical, and this is her dropping into first-person need. The businesslike frame around pure maternal vulnerability is very Moira, and lands as both funny and touching.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you smell that?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Smell what?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That. That smell. It's absolutely putrid.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't smell anything.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it's either you or this place, and I'm fairly certain it's you.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh, so now I smell?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not saying you smell. I'm saying something about you smells.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's the same thing, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It really isn't. One is about your overall essence, the other is situational.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm leaving.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, please do.

jokes.text: 'Excuse me?' / 'I know, it might be the farm.' / '- It's the farm. - Okay.' / '- I smelled something. - It's not me!'
jokes.setup: David's deodorant question has offended Moira
jokes.punchline: Both immediately blame the farm — agreeing it's not Moira — and the rapid cross-talk ('It's not me!' / 'All right, we're leaving!') is the comedic escalation
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the deodorant bit: the rapid cross-talk where both characters simultaneously deflect and excuse is a nice comedic escalation. 'We're leaving! / Relax.' is a solid button.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is like Les Misérables. I'm Jean Valjean stealing a loaf of bread to survive.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you're more like Javert chasing yourself around.

jokes.text: 'Wait. One pizza? What is this, les mis?'
jokes.setup: John brings home food; David sees only one pizza for the whole family
jokes.punchline: David invokes Les Misérables to describe the deprivation of one pizza between four people
jokes.explanation: Excellent David line. The Les Misérables reference for one pizza being split among four is both specific and disproportionate in the best way — David's theatrical suffering over a minor inconvenience given a grand literary/musical frame. Highly quotable, very rewatch-worthy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why don't we all play charades tonight?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Charades? Darling, I don't perform for free. I'm a trained actress with two Gemini nominations.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: If you want me to act out words, you'll need to discuss my rate with my agent. Preferably in writing.

jokes.text: 'Yes, it's a dignified family symposium, David. Not some all-one-can-consume smorgasbord. Don't be an animal, son.'
jokes.setup: David objects to one pizza
jokes.punchline: John's rebuke frames pizza quantity as a matter of dignity — 'dignified family symposium', 'smorgasbord', 'don't be an animal'
jokes.explanation: 'Dignified family symposium' for pizza night is a great John Rose line — he matches David's theatrical register but uses it to defend restraint rather than excess. 'Don't be an animal, son' as a pizza-related admonition is a nice button.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Charades? Darling, I don't do charades. I do interpretive movement, and it requires a Foley artist, a lighting designer, and ideally a small grants committee.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: If we're playing games tonight, I'll need a contract, a rider, and someone to do my hair and makeup.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Also, I assume there will be craft services?

jokes.text: 'I don't think you can afford my quote anymore.' — Moira, about playing charades
jokes.setup: John suggests charades; Moira gracefully declines
jokes.punchline: She frames her charades withdrawal as a rate card issue — she's now too expensive for family game night
jokes.explanation: One of the best Moira lines in the episode. The idea that (a) Moira has a 'quote' for charades, (b) that it has recently increased with the Sunrise Bay booking, and (c) that her family can no longer afford it — is perfectly absurd and perfectly Moira. Economy is exceptional: one sentence captures her inflated self-image, her show-business mentality, and her difficulty separating professional/personal identity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Charades? How delightful. I should warn you, I studied under a dialect coach in Montreal for six months, so my interpretations may be far too nuanced for this sort of thing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm also going to need my own dressing room, and someone to do my hair and makeup beforehand.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And I'll need a contract stipulating that I receive top billing in any photographs taken during gameplay.

jokes.text: 'Just saying it out loud makes it so real.' — reaction to Moira's 'quote' line
jokes.setup: Moira says she can't afford to play charades because her quote has gone up
jokes.punchline: John's understated 'just saying it out loud makes it so real' — accepting this logic with weary love
jokes.explanation: John's resigned acknowledgment of Moira's reality distortion field — treating it not as ridiculous but as confirmation of her success — is sweet and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you're going to New York alone? That's like a beautiful, exotic bird leaving the nest for the first time. You're going to soar, darling. You're going to soar so high that you won't even remember what the nest looked like.

jokes.text: 'The wedding's off.' — David's immediate response to 'I have something to say'
jokes.setup: Patrick announces he has something to say; the family waits
jokes.punchline: David's instant 'The wedding's off' — his catastrophizing instinct firing before anyone has said anything
jokes.explanation: David immediately jumping to 'the wedding's off' before anyone has said anything is a perfect character beat — his catastrophizing anxiety expressed in two words. The timing is impeccable: the audience is set up to expect a serious announcement, and David's panic is the joke. 'No. The wedding is not... Why would the wedding be off?' from Patrick is the ideal straight-man follow.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're leaving tomorrow morning.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Tomorrow? But that's my wedding day!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, we've already booked our flights, and you know how impossible it is to get a decent seat assignment at the last minute. I'm not about to spend my honeymoon in coach.

jokes.text: 'No. The wedding is not... Why would the wedding be off?'
jokes.setup: David has announced 'The wedding's off' apropos of nothing
jokes.punchline: Patrick's baffled 'why would the wedding be off?' — genuinely confused, not having said anything that would lead to this
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man beat: Patrick's confusion validates David's absurdity. The 'Why would the wedding be off?' has the tone of someone who's dealt with David's anxiety spirals before.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not getting on a plane without a premium cabin experience. I don't care if we're fleeing the country.

jokes.text: 'Are you sure you've given this sentencing adequate deliberation?'
jokes.setup: David announces he's staying in Schitt's Creek
jokes.punchline: Moira's word choice: 'sentencing' — framing staying in Schitt's Creek as a legal punishment rather than a choice
jokes.explanation: 'Sentencing' for choosing to stay is a single-word choice that does enormous work — it's Moira's entire relationship with Schitt's Creek (it was a punishment, not a home) compressed into a legal metaphor. The faux-concerned 'adequate deliberation' makes it land even better. High marks for character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm going to spend the rest of my life making you happy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's not going to happen. But I appreciate the sentiment.

jokes.text: 'You take that ember of independence and you keep it burning. Because you, my darling, are destined to be on fire!'
jokes.setup: Moira is encouraging Alexis to go to New York alone
jokes.punchline: The escalation of 'ember of independence' → 'destined to be on fire' is Moira mixing a fire metaphor with a prophecy in a way that sounds threatening as much as encouraging
jokes.explanation: 'Destined to be on fire' as a compliment is wonderfully ambiguous — Moira's mixed metaphors always sound like dire warnings. The commitment to the fire extended metaphor is pure Moira.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Maybe we don't need a big wedding. We could just—

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, no, no. We're having a wedding. A big one. I've already mentally decorated the venue.

jokes.text: 'And we're tied to the third?' / 'Oh Moira...' / 'Am I tied to the third?' / 'Of course you're getting married on the third, David.'
jokes.setup: The wedding day departure conflict has been revealed
jokes.punchline: Moira's immediate instinct is to question whether the wedding date can be changed — 'am I tied to the third?' — as if David's wedding is the flexible variable
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's peak Moira comedy. 'Am I tied to the third?' — with 'the third' being her son's wedding day — is perfectly calibrated Moira self-absorption. She has momentarily forgotten that she cannot reschedule David's wedding around her travel plans. John's 'oh Moira...' and then David's flat 'of course you're getting married on the third, David' — the deadpan correction — are perfect. One of the top moments in the episode.

jokes.text: 'It's just worth noting that the lie-flat seats were only on the evening flight.'
jokes.setup: John says they'll leave first thing the morning after the wedding
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately raises the lie-flat seat issue — she is negotiating her son's wedding departure around airline seat upgrades
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'lie-flat seats' is the joke — not just first class, not just an upgrade, but the specific flatness configuration. Moira framing her son's wedding logistics around airline seating tiers is both appalling and completely consistent with who she is. 'Moira.' / 'It's okay.' / 'Unbelievable.' is the ideal three-part reaction.

jokes.text: Patrick promises to make David happy. David: 'You fuckin' better.'
jokes.setup: Patrick gives a sincere romantic promise to make David happy in Schitt's Creek
jokes.punchline: David's response is simultaneously a love declaration and a threat — 'you fuckin' better' is both tender and perfectly David
jokes.explanation: Three words that might be the most David Rose sentence possible. It's simultaneously an acceptance, a declaration of love, a threat, and a joke — all in one line. The profanity makes it funnier by subverting the romantic register. This is the episode's highest individual impact moment — the room would love this. Maximum character integration and economy.

jokes.text: 'You know, at this point who even needs a wedding, am I right?' / 'Oh, I do. I need a wedding. Big time.'
jokes.setup: David jokes about skipping the wedding since they've already committed to the house
jokes.punchline: Patrick's immediate, earnest counter: 'I need a wedding. Big time.' — a genuine and funny statement of need
jokes.explanation: Patrick's 'I need a wedding. Big time.' is a great closing line — earnest, specific, and funny because 'big time' is such a Patrick-as-straight-man intensifier applied to something sincere. It's a warm button on the episode that also gets a genuine laugh.

tmdb_overview: Moira receives some unexpected news, and David makes a tough decision about where he wants to live.
insight: Finale leans on character chemistry over punchlines; lowest-density season closer yet.
